URL: https://www.britannica.com/topic/positivism

SUMMARY:
Positivism | Definition, History, Theories, & Criticism | Britannica As a philosophical ideology and movement, positivism first assumed its distinctive features in the work of Comte, who also named and systematized the science of sociology. It then developed through several stages known by various names, such as empiriocriticism, logical positivism, and logical empiricism, finally merging, in the mid-20th century, into the already existing tradition known as analytic philosophy. In the United States, John Bernard Stallo, a German-born American philosopher of science (also an educator, jurist, and statesman), developed a positivistic outlook, especially in the philosophy of physics, in his book The Concepts and Theories of Modern Physics (1882), in which he anticipated to a degree some of the general ideas later formulated in the theory of relativity and in quantum mechanics.

**positivism**, in [Western philosophy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Western-philosophy), generally, any system that confines itself to the data of experience and excludes [a priori](https://www.britannica.com/topic/a-priori-knowledge) or [metaphysical](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/metaphysical) speculations. More narrowly, the term designates the thought of the French philosopher [Auguste Comte](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Auguste-Comte) (1798–1857).

As a philosophical [ideology](https://www.britannica.com/topic/ideology-society) and movement, positivism first assumed its distinctive features in the work of [Comte](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Auguste-Comte), who also named and systematized the [science](https://www.britannica.com/science/history-of-science) of [sociology](https://www.britannica.com/topic/sociology). It then developed through several stages known by various names, such as empiriocriticism, [logical positivism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/logical-positivism), and logical [empiricism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/empiricism), finally merging, in the mid-20th century, into the already existing tradition known as [analytic philosophy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/analytic-philosophy).

The basic affirmations of positivism are (1) that all [knowledge](https://www.britannica.com/topic/epistemology) regarding matters of fact is based on the “positive” data of experience and (2) that beyond the realm of fact is that of pure [logic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/logic) and pure [mathematics](https://www.britannica.com/science/mathematics). Those two [disciplines](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/disciplines) were already recognized by the 18th-century Scottish empiricist and [skeptic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/skepticism)[David Hume](https://www.britannica.com/biography/David-Hume) as concerned merely with the “relations of ideas,” and, in a later phase of positivism, they were classified as purely formal sciences. On the negative and critical side, the positivists became noted for their repudiation of [metaphysics](https://www.britannica.com/topic/metaphysics)—i.e., of speculation regarding the nature of [reality](https://www.britannica.com/topic/reality-of-the-external-world) that radically goes beyond any possible [evidence](https://www.britannica.com/topic/evidence-law) that could either support or refute such “transcendent” knowledge claims. In its basic ideological posture, positivism is thus worldly, [secular](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/secular), antitheological, and antimetaphysical. Strict [adherence](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/adherence) to the testimony of observation and experience is the all-important [imperative](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/imperative) of positivism. That imperative was reflected also in the contributions by positivists to [ethics](https://www.britannica.com/topic/ethics-philosophy) and [moral](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/moral) philosophy, which were generally [utilitarian](https://www.britannica.com/topic/utilitarianism-philosophy) to the extent that something like “the greatest happiness for the greatest number of people” was their [ethical](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/ethical) maxim. It is notable, in this connection, that Comte was the founder of a short-lived [religion](https://www.britannica.com/topic/study-of-religion), in which the object of worship was not the deity of the monotheistic faiths but humanity.

[![Image 24: Georg Christoph Lichtenberg](https://cdn.britannica.com/96/133796-050-05AC0D44/statue-Georg-Christoph-Lichtenberg-Gottingen-Ger.jpg?w=300)](https://cdn.britannica.com/96/133796-050-05AC0D44/statue-Georg-Christoph-Lichtenberg-Gottingen-Ger.jpg)

[Georg Christoph Lichtenberg](https://cdn.britannica.com/96/133796-050-05AC0D44/statue-Georg-Christoph-Lichtenberg-Gottingen-Ger.jpg)Georg Christoph Lichtenberg, statue in Göttingen, Germany.(more)

There are distinct anticipations of positivism in ancient [philosophy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy). Although the relationship of [Protagoras](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Protagoras-Greek-philosopher)—a 5th-century-bce[Sophist](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sophist-philosophy)—for example, to later positivistic thought was only a distant one, there was a much more pronounced similarity in the classical skeptic [Sextus Empiricus](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Sextus-Empiricus), who lived at the turn of the 3rd century ce, and in [Pierre Bayle](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Pierre-Bayle), his 17th-century reviver. Moreover, the [medieval](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/medieval)[nominalist](https://www.britannica.com/topic/nominalism)[William of Ockham](https://www.britannica.com/biography/William-of-Ockham) had clear [affinities](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/affinities) with modern positivism. An 18th-century forerunner who had much in common with the positivistic antimetaphysics of the following century was the German thinker [Georg Lichtenberg.](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Georg-Christoph-Lichtenberg)

The proximate roots of positivism, however, clearly lie in the French [Enlightenment](https://www.britannica.com/event/Enlightenment-European-history), which stressed the clear light of [reason](https://www.britannica.com/topic/reason), and in 18th-century British empiricism, particularly that of Hume and of Bishop [George Berkeley](https://www.britannica.com/biography/George-Berkeley), which stressed the role of sense experience. Comte was influenced specifically by the Enlightenment [Encyclopaedists](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Encyclopedie) (such as [Denis Diderot](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Denis-Diderot), [Jean d’Alembert](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Jean-Le-Rond-dAlembert), and others) and, especially in his social thinking, was decisively influenced by the founder of French [socialism](https://www.britannica.com/money/socialism), [Claude-Henri, comte de Saint-Simon](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Henri-de-Saint-Simon), whose [disciple](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/disciple) he had been in his early years and from whom the very [designation](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/designation)_positivism_ stems.

The social positivism of Comte and Mill
---------------------------------------

[![Image 25: Auguste Comte](https://cdn.britannica.com/78/23678-050-1DCF79A2/Auguste-Comte-Tony-Toullion-Bibliotheque-Nationale-Paris.jpg?w=300)](https://cdn.britannica.com/78/23678-050-1DCF79A2/Auguste-Comte-Tony-Toullion-Bibliotheque-Nationale-Paris.jpg)

[Auguste Comte](https://cdn.britannica.com/78/23678-050-1DCF79A2/Auguste-Comte-Tony-Toullion-Bibliotheque-Nationale-Paris.jpg)Auguste Comte, drawing by Tony Toullion, 19th century; in the Bibliothèque Nationale, Paris.(more)

Comte’s positivism was posited on the assertion of a so-called law of the three phases (or stages) of [intellectual](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/intellectual) development. There is a parallel, as Comte saw it, between the evolution of thought patterns in the entire [history](https://www.britannica.com/topic/history) of humankind, on the one hand, and in the history of an individual’s development from infancy to adulthood, on the other. In the first, or so-called [theological](https://www.britannica.com/topic/theology), stage, natural phenomena are explained as the results of supernatural or divine powers. It matters not whether the [religion](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy-of-religion) is [polytheistic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/polytheism) or [monotheistic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/monotheism); in either case, miraculous powers or wills are believed to produce the observed events. This stage was criticized by Comte as [anthropomorphic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/anthropomorphism)—i.e., as resting on all-too-human [analogies](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/analogies). Generally, animistic explanations—made in terms of the volitions of soul-like beings operating behind the appearances—are rejected as primitive projections of unverifiable entities.

The second phase, called metaphysical, is in some cases merely a depersonalized theology: the observable processes of nature are assumed to arise from impersonal powers, occult qualities, vital forces, or [entelechies](https://www.britannica.com/topic/entelechy) (internal perfecting principles). In other instances, the realm of observable facts is considered as an imperfect copy or imitation of eternal ideas, as in [Plato](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Plato)’s [metaphysics](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/metaphysics) of pure [forms](https://www.britannica.com/topic/form-philosophy). Again, Comte charged that no genuine explanations result; questions concerning ultimate reality, first causes, or absolute beginnings are thus declared to be absolutely unanswerable. The metaphysical quest can lead only to the conclusion expressed by the German biologist and physiologist [Emil du Bois-Reymond](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Emil-Heinrich-Du-Bois-Reymond): “Ignoramus et ignorabimus” (Latin: “We are and shall be ignorant”). It is a deception through verbal devices and the fruitless rendering of [concepts](https://www.britannica.com/topic/concept) as real things.

![Image 26](https://cdn.britannica.com/marketing/BlueThistle.webp)

The sort of fruitfulness that it lacks can be achieved only in the third phase, the [scientific](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy-of-science), or “positive,” phase—hence the title of Comte’s magnum opus: _[Cours de philosophie positive](https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-Positive-Philosophy-of-Auguste-Comte)_ (1830–42)—because it claims to be concerned only with positive facts. The task of the sciences, and of knowledge in general, is to study the facts and regularities of nature and society and to formulate the regularities as (descriptive) [laws](https://www.britannica.com/topic/natural-law); explanations of phenomena can consist in no more than the subsuming of special cases under general laws. Humankind reached full maturity of thought only after abandoning the pseudoexplanations of the theological and [metaphysical](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/metaphysical) phases and substituting an unrestricted adherence to [scientific method](https://www.britannica.com/science/scientific-method).

In his three stages Comte combined what he considered to be an account of the historical order of development with a logical analysis of the leveled structure of the sciences. By arranging the six basic and pure sciences one upon the other in a pyramid, Comte prepared the way for logical positivism to “reduce” each level to the one below it. He placed at the fundamental level the science that does not presuppose any other sciences—viz., mathematics—and then ordered the levels above it in such a way that each science depends upon, and makes use of, the sciences below it on the scale: thus, [arithmetic](https://www.britannica.com/science/arithmetic) and the [theory of numbers](https://www.britannica.com/science/number-theory) are declared to be presuppositions for [geometry](https://www.britannica.com/science/geometry) and [mechanics](https://www.britannica.com/science/mechanics), [astronomy](https://www.britannica.com/science/astronomy), [physics](https://www.britannica.com/science/physics-science), [chemistry](https://www.britannica.com/science/chemistry), [biology](https://www.britannica.com/science/biology) (including [physiology](https://www.britannica.com/science/physiology)), and [sociology](https://www.britannica.com/topic/sociology). Each higher-level science, in turn, adds to the knowledge content of the science or sciences on the levels below, thus enriching this content by successive specialization. [Psychology](https://www.britannica.com/science/psychology), which was not founded as a formal [discipline](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/discipline) until the late 19th century, was not included in Comte’s system of the sciences. Anticipating some ideas of 20th-century [behaviourism](https://www.britannica.com/science/behaviourism-psychology) and [physicalism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/physicalistic-materialism), Comte assumed that psychology, such as it was in his day, should become a branch of biology (especially of [brain](https://www.britannica.com/science/brain) neurophysiology), on the one hand, and of sociology, on the other. As the “father” of sociology, Comte maintained that the [social sciences](https://www.britannica.com/topic/social-science) should proceed from observations to general laws, very much as (in his view) physics and chemistry do. He was skeptical of [introspection](https://www.britannica.com/topic/introspection) in psychology, being convinced that in attending to one’s own mental states, these states would be irretrievably altered and distorted. In thus insisting on the necessity of objective observation, he was close to the basic principle of the [methodology](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/methodology) of 20th-century behaviourism.

Among Comte’s [disciples](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/disciples) or sympathizers were [Cesare Lombroso](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Cesare-Lombroso), an Italian [psychiatrist](https://www.britannica.com/science/psychiatry) and [criminologist](https://www.britannica.com/science/criminology), and [Paul-Emile Littré](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Paul-Emile-Littre), [J.-E. Renan](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Ernest-Renan), and Louis Weber.

[![Image 27: John Stuart Mill](https://cdn.britannica.com/68/99068-050-BF33B0EB/John-Stuart-Mill-carte-de-visite-1884.jpg?w=300)](https://cdn.britannica.com/68/99068-050-BF33B0EB/John-Stuart-Mill-carte-de-visite-1884.jpg)

[John Stuart Mill](https://cdn.britannica.com/68/99068-050-BF33B0EB/John-Stuart-Mill-carte-de-visite-1884.jpg)John Stuart Mill, 1884.(more)

Despite some basic disagreements with Comte, the 19th-century English philosopher [John Stuart Mill](https://www.britannica.com/biography/John-Stuart-Mill), also a logician and [economist](https://www.britannica.com/money/economics), must be regarded as one of the outstanding positivists of his century. In his _[System of Logic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/A-System-of-Logic)_ (1843), he developed a thoroughly empiricist [theory of knowledge](https://www.britannica.com/topic/epistemology) and of scientific reasoning, going even so far as to regard logic and mathematics as [empirical](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/empirical) (though very general) sciences. The broadly [synthetic](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/synthetic) philosopher [Herbert Spencer](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Herbert-Spencer), author of a doctrine of the “unknowable” and of a general evolutionary philosophy, was, next to Mill, an outstanding exponent of a positivistic orientation.

The critical positivism of Mach and Avenarius
=============================================

The influences of Hume and of Comte were also [manifest](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/manifest) in important developments in German positivism, just prior to [World War I](https://www.britannica.com/event/World-War-I). The outstanding representatives of this school were [Ernst Mach](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Ernst-Mach)—a [philosophical](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy-of-nature) critic of the physics of [Isaac Newton](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Isaac-Newton), an original thinker as a physicist, and a historian of mechanics, [thermodynamics](https://www.britannica.com/science/thermodynamics), and [optics](https://www.britannica.com/science/optics)—and [Richard Avenarius](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Richard-Avenarius), founder of a [philosophy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy) known as empiriocriticism.

Mach, in the introductory chapter of his book _[Beiträge zur Analyse der Empfindungen](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Contributions-to-the-Analysis-of-the-Sensations)_ (1886; _Contributions to the Analysis of the Sensations_), reviving Humean antimetaphysics, contended that all factual knowledge consists of a [conceptual](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/conceptual) organization and elaboration of what is given in the elements—i.e., in the [data](https://www.britannica.com/topic/sense-data) of immediate experience. Very much in keeping with the spirit of Comte, he [repudiated](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/repudiated) the [transcendental idealism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/transcendental-idealism) of [Immanuel Kant](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Immanuel-Kant). From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Empiricist philosophical theory

For other uses, see [Positivism (disambiguation)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism_(disambiguation) "Positivism (disambiguation)").

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Auguste_Comte.jpg/250px-Auguste_Comte.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Auguste_Comte.jpg)

[Auguste Comte](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Auguste_Comte "Auguste Comte"), the founder of modern positivism

**Positivism** is a [philosophical school](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philosophical_school "Philosophical school") that holds that all genuine knowledge is either [true by definition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tautology_(logic) "Tautology (logic)") or [positive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positive_statement "Positive statement")– meaning _[a posteriori](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/A\_posteriori "A posteriori")_ facts derived by [reason](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reason "Reason") and [logic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logic "Logic") from [sensory experience](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perception "Perception").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-MacionisGerber7ed-1)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-Larrain1979p197-2) Other [ways of knowing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epistemology "Epistemology"), such as [intuition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intuition "Intuition"), [introspection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Introspection "Introspection"), or [religious faith](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Religious_epistemology "Religious epistemology"), are rejected or [considered meaningless](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Verificationism "Verificationism").

Although the positivist approach has been a recurrent theme in the history of Western thought, modern positivism was first articulated in the early 19th century by [Auguste Comte](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Auguste_Comte "Auguste Comte").[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-Cohen,_Manion_et_al.-3)[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-4) His school of [sociological](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sociology "Sociology") positivism holds that society, like the physical world, operates according to [scientific laws](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_law "Scientific law").[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-5) After Comte, positivist schools arose in [logic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logic "Logic"), [psychology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychology "Psychology"), [economics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economics "Economics"), [historiography](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Historiography "Historiography"), and other fields of thought. Generally, positivists attempted to introduce scientific methods to their respective fields. Since the turn of the 20th century, positivism, although still popular, has declined under criticism within the social sciences by [antipositivists](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antipositivist "Antipositivist") and [critical theorists](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Critical_theorist "Critical theorist"), among others, for its alleged [scientism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientism "Scientism"), [reductionism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reductionism "Reductionism"), overgeneralizations, and methodological limitations. Positivism also exerted an unusual influence on [Kardecism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kardecist_spiritism "Kardecist spiritism").[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-6)[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-7)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-8)

Etymology
---------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Positivism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The English noun _positivism_ in this meaning was imported in the 19th century from the French word _positivisme_, derived from _positif_ in its philosophical sense of 'imposed on the mind by experience'. The corresponding adjective ([Latin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): _positivus_) has been used in a similar sense to discuss law ([positive law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positive_law "Positive law") compared to [natural law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_law "Natural law")) since the time of [Chaucer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer").[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-9)

Background
----------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Positivism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

[Kieran Egan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kieran_Egan_(philosopher) "Kieran Egan (philosopher)") argues that positivism can be traced to the philosophy side of what [Plato](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plato "Plato") described as the quarrel between [philosophy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") and [poetry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poetry "Poetry"), later reformulated by [Wilhelm Dilthey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wilhelm_Dilthey "Wilhelm Dilthey") as a quarrel between the [natural sciences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_sciences "Natural sciences") ([German](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_language "German language"): _Naturwissenschaften_) and the [human sciences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human_sciences "Human sciences") (_Geisteswissenschaften_).[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-Egan1997p114-10)[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-11)[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-Wallace2008p27-12)

In the early nineteenth century, massive advances in the natural sciences encouraged philosophers to apply scientific methods to other fields. Thinkers such as [Henri de Saint-Simon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henri_de_Saint-Simon "Henri de Saint-Simon"), [Pierre-Simon Laplace](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pierre-Simon_Laplace "Pierre-Simon Laplace") and [Auguste Comte](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Auguste_Comte "Auguste Comte") believed that the [scientific method](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method"), the circular dependence of theory and observation, must replace [metaphysics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metaphysics "Metaphysics") in the [history](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History "History") of thought.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-Hobsbawm-13)

Positivism in the social sciences
---------------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Positivism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Positivism in the social sciences")]

### Comte's positivism

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Positivism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Comte's positivism")]

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Comte%2C_%27Cours_de_philosophie_positive%27_Wellcome_L0016061.jpg/250px-Comte%2C_%27Cours_de_philosophie_positive%27_Wellcome_L0016061.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Comte,_%27Cours_de_philosophie_positive%27_Wellcome_L0016061.jpg)

Comte first laid out his theory of positivism in _[The Course in Positive Philosophy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Course\_in\_Positive\_Philosophy "The Course in Positive Philosophy")_.

[Auguste Comte](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Auguste_Comte "Auguste Comte") (1798–1857) first described the epistemological perspective of positivism in _[The Course in Positive Philosophy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Course\_in\_Positive\_Philosophy "The Course in Positive Philosophy")_, a series of texts published between 1830 and 1842. These texts were followed in 1844 by _[A General View of Positivism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/A\_General\_View\_of\_Positivism "A General View of Positivism")_ (published in French 1848, English in 1865). The first three volumes of the _Course_ dealt chiefly with the physical sciences already in existence ([mathematics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), [astronomy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"), [physics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Physics "Physics"), [chemistry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"), [biology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biology "Biology")), whereas the latter two emphasized the inevitable coming of [social science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_science "Social science"). Observing the circular dependence of theory and observation in science, and classifying the sciences in this way, Comte may be regarded as the first [philosopher of science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philosopher_of_science "Philosopher of science") in the modern sense of the term.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-plato.stanford.edu-14)[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-15) For him, the physical sciences had necessarily to arrive first, before humanity could adequately channel its efforts into the most challenging and complex "Queen science" of human society itself. His _View of Positivism_ therefore set out to define the empirical goals of sociological method:

> The most important thing to determine was the natural order in which the sciences stand—not how they can be made to stand, but how they must stand, irrespective of the wishes of any one. ... This Comte accomplished by taking as the criterion of the position of each the degree of what he called "positivity," which is simply the degree to which the phenomena can be exactly determined. This, as may be readily seen, is also a measure of their relative complexity, since the exactness of a science is in inverse proportion to its complexity. The degree of exactness or positivity is, moreover, that to which it can be subjected to mathematical demonstration, and therefore mathematics, which is not itself a concrete science, is the general gauge by which the position of every science is to be determined. Generalizing thus, Comte found that there were five great groups of phenomena of equal classificatory value but of successively decreasing positivity. To these he gave the names astronomy, physics, chemistry, biology, and sociology.
> 
> 
> 
> — [Lester F. Ward](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lester_F._Ward "Lester F. Ward"), _The Outlines of Sociology_ (1898), [[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-DinW-16)

Comte offered an [account of social evolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_evolutionism "Social evolutionism"), proposing that society undergoes three phases in its quest for the truth according to a general "[law of three stages](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Law_of_three_stages "Law of three stages")". Comte intended to develop a secular-scientific ideology in the wake of European [secularisation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secularisation "Secularisation").

Comte's stages were (1) the _[theological](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theological "Theological")_, (2) the _[metaphysical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metaphysics "Metaphysics")_, and (3) the _positive_.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-17) The theological phase of man was based on whole-hearted belief in all things with reference to [God](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/God "God"). God, Comte says, had reigned supreme over human existence pre-[Enlightenment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"). Humanity's place in society was governed by its association with the divine presences and with the church. The theological phase deals with humankind's accepting the doctrines of the church (or place of worship) rather than relying on its rational powers to explore basic questions about existence. It dealt with the restrictions put in place by the religious organization at the time and the total acceptance of any "fact" adduced for society to believe.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-18)

Comte describes the metaphysical phase of humanity as the time since the [Enlightenment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"), a time steeped in logical [rationalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rationalism "Rationalism"), to the time right after the [French Revolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"). This second phase states that the universal rights of humanity are most important. The central idea is that humanity is invested with certain rights that must be respected. In this phase, democracies and dictators rose and fell in attempts to maintain the innate rights of humanity.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positivism#cite_note-19)

--- SOURCE 5: How Positivism Shaped Our Understanding of Reality ---
URL: https://meridianuniversity.edu/content/how-positivism-shaped-our-understanding-of-reality

SUMMARY:
Positivism is a philosophical approach asserting that authentic knowledge is derived from sensory experience, preferably through scientific methods. Positivism in Psychology One of the most commendable contributions of positivism to psychology is its systematic research approach. Positivism has allowed researchers to systematically investigate various psychological phenomena by emphasizing replicable experiments and observation-based evidence. Positivism, emphasizing empirical evidence, has given rise to numerous evidence-based theories in psychology. Positivism, with its staunch emphasis on empirical evidence and quantitative measures, is criticized for oversimplifying intricate human behaviors and emotions. While positivism emphasizes empirical observation and quantifiable data, other approaches in psychology delve deeper into the subjective realm of human experience. While positivism has played a pivotal role in shaping the trajectory of psychological research, it is not the only approach.

How Positivism Shaped Our Understanding of Reality
==================================================

Meridian Team

**How Positivism Shaped Our Understanding of Reality**
------------------------------------------------------

![Image 2: Portrait of Auguste Comte, symbolizing the beginnings of positivism.](https://web-cdn.meridianuniversity.edu/site-content-images/content-library/how-positivism-shaped-our-understanding-of-reality-image-one.webp)
Auguste Comte, the father of positivism, whose ideas revolutionized our understanding of science and reality.

**Introduction**
----------------

### **Definition of Positivism**

_Positivism_ is a philosophical approach asserting that authentic knowledge is derived from sensory experience, preferably through scientific methods. In its essence, positivism advocates that our knowledge of reality is firmly rooted in observable and measurable phenomena rather than abstract theorization or metaphysical speculation.

### **Brief Historical Context**

The term positivism traces its origins to the early nineteenth century, significantly influenced by thinkers such as Auguste Comte and Jeremy Bentham. [According to the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy](https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/legal-positivism/), Comte, often termed the father of sociology, posited that human societies progress in stages, with the scientific or "positive" stage being the pinnacle.

[Concurrently, Bentham, alongside John Stuart Mill, laid the groundwork for "legal positivism,"](https://philpapers.org/rec/ZTARST-2) distinguishing between the law as it exists and what it ought to be, which contrasts with the natural law doctrine. Moreover, with figures like De Saint Simon advocating for a new social order rooted in scientific reasoning, the emergence of positivism fundamentally shaped the course of intellectual history.

### **Importance of the Concept in the Development of Scientific Thinking**

The rise of positivism in the nineteenth century marked a decisive shift in how knowledge was pursued and validated. By advocating scientific methods, positivism played an instrumental role in the evolution of the social sciences. In the realm of natural sciences, positivism's emphasis on empirical evidence fostered rigorous methodologies and objective analysis.

Furthermore, logical positivism, which emerged from the Vienna Circle, [argued that meaningful statements must either be empirically verifiable or analytically true](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1080/14640748908401199), further refining the positivist approach. This thought stream influenced many academic institutions, with notable publications from Oxford University Press and Cambridge University Press contributing to its dissemination and discussion.

The impact of positivism extends beyond academia. [The motto "Ordem e Progresso" (Order and Progress) on Brazil's flag embodies](https://www.britannica.com/topic/flag-of-Brazil) positivist principles, underscoring the concept's influence in shaping national identities and advocating for a social order based on reason and empirical knowledge.

**Historical Development of Positivism**
----------------------------------------

### **Early Roots: Auguste Comte and the Foundation of Positivism**

The records of history regard Auguste Comte as a revolutionary thinker, especially in the context of positivism. Born amidst the upheavals of post-revolutionary France, the surrounding chaos shaped Comte's perspectives. Seeking order and reason, he laid down the philosophy of positivism, emphasizing the necessity of empirical evidence in human knowledge.

Comte's positivism diverged from traditional epistemologies, emphasizing that true knowledge emanated from observed facts and the relationships among them. In the intricate tapestry of human intellectual development, Comte introduced the idea of societies evolving through three distinct stages: the theological, the metaphysical, and the positive. [The final stage—positive—represented a society where knowledge is rooted in scientific methods and empirical inquiries](https://philpapers.org/rec/MILACA-8).

### **The Evolution of Positivist Thinking in the 19th and Early 20th Century**

The 19th century was pivotal for positivist thinking. An age of revolutions, both industrial and political, the era demanded rigorous methodologies to understand the rapidly changing world. Enter Jeremy Bentham, whose work in legal studies was transformative.

[Bentham's legal positivism stipulated that laws are human creations designed to serve the collective good](https://philpapers.org/rec/HARTCO-9). This was revolutionary, as it redefined the foundation of legal systems, grounding them in human intent and consensus rather than divine ordination.

Equally influential was De Saint Simon, who envisaged a society steered by positivist principles. In his utopia, societies would dispense with outdated superstitions and biases, instead embracing a framework rooted in scientific knowledge and empirical evidence. It was a compelling vision of a world where science and rationality would craft a harmonious social order.

### **Key Figures and Their Contributions**

The 20th century saw positivism's principles refined and debated fiercely. Central to this discourse was the Vienna Circle, a group of philosophers and scientists who pioneered logical positivism. Rudolf Carnap, a key figure in this movement, argued passionately that meaningful statements should be empirically verifiable or logically consistent. [For Carnap and his peers, this was not just a theoretical exercise but a call to arms against metaphysical obscurantism](https://philpapers.org/rec/CARTUO-21).

However, positivism's influence was not confined to the European mainland. The venerable institutions of Oxford and Cambridge in the UK became hotbeds for rigorous discussions on positivism. Through their prestigious publishing arms—Oxford University Press and Cambridge University Press—both universities endorsed and critiqued positivist methodologies, testifying to the philosophy's profound impact.

In the natural sciences, positivism's influence was nothing short of transformative. As researchers delved into the mysteries of atoms and galaxies, they adhered to the rigorous empirical methods backed by positivism. In the domain of social sciences, the philosophy found resonance among researchers keen to study societies and individuals through quantifiable and observable data, sidestepping unfounded speculation.

By the end of the 20th century, while positivism faced critiques and modifications, its legacy was undeniable. It had indelibly shaped multiple disciplines, from law to sociology to natural sciences, molding the modern scientific temperament.

**Key Principles of Positivism**
--------------------------------

### **The Primacy of Observation and Empirical Methods**

Positivism's foundational pillar is the belief that knowledge emanates primarily from observation and empirical methods. This conviction has its roots in the perspective that proper understanding of the world around us requires tangible, sensory-driven evidence, as opposed to abstract reasoning or speculation. It is not merely enough to theorize or philosophize; a claim to earn the positivist's trust must be demonstrable and observable.

For instance, a positivist would not just accept anecdotal experiences or ancient texts as evidence if one were to make a claim about a specific medical condition. They would demand recent clinical trials, patient observations, and concrete data. [This rigorous insistence on empirical evidence](https://philpapers.org/rec/COMAGV-4) has directly influenced the methodologies of modern scientific research, ensuring that conclusions drawn are based on direct or indirect observations of the world.

### **Rejection of Metaphysics and Speculation**

Another central tenet of positivism is its rejection of metaphysical assertions and speculative reasoning. This stance has made positivism somewhat controversial, especially when pitted against philosophical traditions relying heavily on metaphysics. For positivists, if a concept or idea cannot be directly observed, measured, or tested empirically, it remains outside the domain of genuine knowledge.

Consider, for example, abstract concepts like 'soul' or 'afterlife.' While these notions hold significance in various cultural, religious, or philosophical contexts, positivism would refrain from making claims about their existence or nature since they are beyond empirical examination. [This stringent boundary around what constitutes valid knowledge](https://philpapers.org/rec/AYELTA-2) ensures clarity but also spurs debates about the limits of human understanding.

### **The Search for Universal Laws**

The ambition to uncover universal laws, inspired by the success of the natural sciences, underscores positivism's approach. Just as physicists might seek laws governing physical phenomena, a positivist sociologist aims to discern patterns or laws in human behavior. For instance, while individual human actions might seem spontaneous or unpredictable, positivists believe that, when observed in large numbers, [these actions might reveal consistent patterns, reflecting underlying societal laws](https://www.rep.routledge.com/articles/biographical/durkheim-emile-1858-1917/v-1).
